figures.caption: Fig. 1: Training, calibration, and online monitoring workflow of PL-CUSUM. (a) Window segmentation, (b) lag-embedded point clouds, (c) spectral-input and scale selection, (d) PL spectral features and whitening projection, and (e) online Page–CUSUM recursion.

Fig. 2: Positive PL spectral separation in the tree family. The horizontal axis is the tree order m, and the vertical axis is the feature separation between the path tree and the star tree; the blue curve gives the positive PL spectral separation, and the gray dashed line gives the Betti separation.

Fig. 3: Separation-scaling experiment in a finite-support PL model. The left panel compares the local information I with the squared ridge-whitened separation γ2ρ; the right panel compares the median alarm delay of oracle LLR-CUSUM with log(N/α)/γ2ρ. The shaded region marks the main range in which the local expansion is used.

Fig. 4: Effect of the Phase I training sample size on plug-in PL-CUSUM. The left panel shows the single-step increment bias eemp versus √ p/Ntr; the right panel shows the ratio between the censored median delay of plug-in PL score CUSUM and that of oracle PL score CUSUM, with color indicating the hit fraction.

Fig. 5: Nonlinear geometric stress test with standardized windows. The left panel compares the ridge-whitened separations of the positive PL spectrum and second-order moment features; the right panel compares the censored median delays of the corresponding CUSUM recursions, with crosses indicating hit fractions below 0.8.

Fig. 6: Hyperparameter sensitivity of PL-CUSUM. The top row varies the window length w, and the bottom row varies the ridge parameter ρ; the left column shows the ratio between the post-change mean increment and the control limit, and the right column shows the censored median delay.

Table 1. Method-level aggregate results over the 38 evaluation tasks. Pre controlled is the number of tasks with Pre≤ 0.05. SDR qualified is the number of tasks satisfying both Pre≤ 0.05 and SDR≥ 0.999. Delay is summarized only over SDR-qualified tasks.
Fig. 7: Task-family coverage heatmap in the real-data experiments. Color indicates the pass fraction of each method within each task family, where a pass is defined as Pre≤ 0.05 and SDR≥ 0.999.

Fig. 8: Normalized online PL-CUSUM trajectories in real-data case studies. The vertical axis is Sn/η; the horizontal dashed line is the control limit, the gray vertical line is the true change point, and the red vertical line is the first alarm.
Fig. 9: Pre- and post-change PL projection scores in real-data case studies. Black horizontal lines indicate medians, and the red dashed line connects the two state medians.

Table 2: Data sources for the formal experimental tasks. Task-specific file versions, splits, preprocessing scripts, and parameter files for the formal repeated experiments are provided in the GitHub repository.
Table 3. Baseline method definitions. All methods use the same training, calibration, and evaluation splits, and their control limits are set under the same pre-change false-alarm budget.
